title: Test Revision — Motion, Forces, Newton's Laws & Waves
title_en: Test Revision — Motion, Forces, Newton's Laws & Waves

sections.heading: What this test covers
sections.content:
This is a complete revision guide for your physics test. It is split into two halves: Kinematics (describing how things move — graphs, distance, speed, acceleration) and Dynamics (why things move — forces, weight, friction, springs and Newton's laws).

Work through each section, study the diagrams, and try the worked examples yourself before reading the answer. Mini-quizzes appear along the way so you can check yourself. Everything you need for the ten exam objectives is here.

sections.heading: 1. Reading a graph: gradient and area
sections.content:
Two pieces of information can be pulled out of almost any graph:
  • The gradient (slope) tells you how steep the line is. It is calculated as $\text{gradient} = \dfrac{\Delta y}{\Delta x}$ — the change in the y-value divided by the change in the x-value ("rise over run"). A steeper line = a bigger gradient.
  • The area under the line (between the line and the x-axis) is found by working out the area of the shape (rectangles + triangles).
The clever part of physics is that the gradient and the area each represent a real physical quantity, depending on what is on the axes.

sections.heading: Which quantity comes from which graph?
sections.content:
This little table is worth memorising:
  • Distance–time (d–t): gradient = speed/velocity. (No useful area.)
  • Velocity–time (v–t): gradient = acceleration; area under the line = distance travelled.
  • Acceleration–time (a–t): area under the line = change in velocity.
So if you are ever asked "how do I get distance from a velocity–time graph?" the answer is always: find the area underneath.

sections.heading: 2. Distance, displacement, speed, velocity, acceleration
sections.content:
Some of these words sound similar but mean different things. The key idea is scalar (size only) vs vector (size + direction).
  • Distance — how far you actually travelled along your path. Scalar. Unit: metre (m).
  • Displacement — the straight-line distance and direction from start to finish. Vector. Unit: metre (m).
  • Speed — how fast (distance ÷ time). Scalar. Unit: m/s.
  • Velocity — speed in a given direction (displacement ÷ time). Vector. Unit: m/s.
  • Acceleration — how quickly velocity changes. Vector. Unit: m/s² (metres per second, per second).
If you walk all the way around a running track and stop where you started, your distance is one lap but your displacement is zero.

sections.heading: Converting m/s ↔ km/h
sections.content:
You will be asked to switch between the two speed units.
  • m/s → km/h: multiply by 3.6
  • km/h → m/s: divide by 3.6
Where does 3.6 come from? There are 1000 m in a km and 3600 s in an hour, and $\frac{3600}{1000} = 3.6$. A quick sanity check: a person walks at about 1.4 m/s ≈ 5 km/h, which feels right.

sections.heading: Worked example: unit conversion
sections.content:
(a) Convert 25 m/s into km/h. → $25 \times 3.6 = \mathbf{90}$ km/h.
(b) Convert 72 km/h into m/s. → $72 \div 3.6 = \mathbf{20}$ m/s.

Tip: the number in km/h is always the larger one, so if your km/h answer comes out smaller than the m/s value, you divided when you should have multiplied.

sections.heading: 3. Formulas — uniform motion (constant speed)
sections.content: "Uniform" motion means the speed never changes. The basic triangle relationship is:

sections.heading: Formulas — accelerated motion
sections.content: When the velocity changes, use these. Here $u$ = starting (initial) velocity, $v$ = final velocity, $a$ = acceleration, $t$ = time, $d$ = distance:
sections.latex: a = \frac{v-u}{t} \qquad v = u + a\,t \qquad d = \left(\frac{u+v}{2}\right)t \qquad d = u\,t + \tfrac{1}{2}a\,t^2

sections.heading: Worked example: accelerated motion
sections.content:
A car starts from rest ($u = 0$) and accelerates at $a = 2\text{ m/s}^2$ for $t = 5\text{ s}$.

Final velocity: $v = u + at = 0 + (2)(5) = \mathbf{10}$ m/s.
Distance travelled: $d = ut + \tfrac{1}{2}at^2 = 0 + \tfrac{1}{2}(2)(5^2) = \mathbf{25}$ m.

Check with the average-velocity formula: $d = \left(\frac{0+10}{2}\right)(5) = 25$ m. ✓

sections.heading: 4. Average speed (and the three motion graphs)
sections.content:
Average speed is never "add the speeds and halve them". It is always:

$\text{average speed} = \dfrac{\text{total distance}}{\text{total time}}$

The three graphs you must recognise are the distance–time (d–t), velocity–time (v–t) and acceleration–time (a–t) graphs — covered next.

sections.heading: Worked example: average speed
sections.content:
A cyclist rides 120 m in 8 s, then a further 80 m in 12 s.

Total distance $= 120 + 80 = 200$ m. Total time $= 8 + 12 = 20$ s.
$\text{average speed} = \dfrac{200}{20} = \mathbf{10}$ m/s.

Notice this is not the average of the two separate speeds (15 m/s and ≈6.7 m/s).

sections.heading: The distance–time (d–t) graph
sections.content: On a d–t graph, the gradient is the speed. A steeper line means a faster object. A horizontal (flat) line means the object is stationary — its distance is not changing. A curved line means the speed is changing.

sections.heading: The velocity–time (v–t) graph
sections.content:
This is the most important graph. On a v–t graph:
  • the gradient is the acceleration (a flat line = constant velocity = zero acceleration);
  • the area under the line is the distance travelled.
To get the distance, split the area into triangles and rectangles and add them up.

A car speeds up from rest to 20 m/s in 4 s. What is its acceleration?
On a distance–time graph, what does the gradient (slope) represent?
A runner covers 400 m in 50 s. What is the average speed?

sections.heading: 5. Reading and constructing graphs (Y–X and Y–T)
sections.content:
Whether the x-axis is a distance (a Y–X graph) or time (a Y–T graph), the skills are the same.

Reading a graph: go up from the x-axis value until you hit the line, then across to read the y-value (or the reverse). Always check the units and the scale of each axis first.

Constructing a graph:
  • Put the quantity you control on the x-axis, the one you measure on the y-axis.
  • Label each axis with its quantity and unit, e.g. "Time (s)".
  • Choose a sensible, even scale that uses most of the paper.
  • Plot each point with a small cross, then draw a single smooth line of best fit — do not just join the dots zig-zag.

sections.heading: 6. Free fall and terminal velocity
sections.content:
When an object falls, two forces act: its weight pulling it down, and air resistance (drag) pushing up.

At the start, weight is much bigger than drag, so the object accelerates (speeds up). As it speeds up, the air resistance grows. Eventually air resistance becomes equal to the weight: the forces are balanced, the resultant force is zero, so there is no more acceleration. The object then falls at a constant speed called the terminal velocity. (You do not need a formula for this — just the idea.)

sections.heading: 7. Mass and weight
sections.content:
These are constantly mixed up — keep them separate:
  • Mass is the amount of matter in an object. It is a scalar, measured in kilograms (kg), and is the same everywhere in the universe.
  • Weight is the force of gravity pulling on that mass. It is a vector (points down), measured in newtons (N), and changes if gravity changes (you weigh less on the Moon).

sections.heading: Weight formula
sections.content: Weight = mass × gravitational field strength. On Earth $g \approx 9.81\text{ N/kg}$ (often rounded to $10$ in class):

sections.heading: Worked example: weight
sections.content:
Find the weight of a 60 kg student on Earth ($g = 9.81\text{ N/kg}$).

$W = m \times g = 60 \times 9.81 = \mathbf{588.6}$ N (about 589 N).

Using the classroom value $g = 10$ gives $600$ N — close enough, but always use the value the question gives you.

sections.heading: Working with vectors in one dimension
sections.content: Along a single straight line, you do not need arrows on paper — you use + and − signs instead. Pick one direction as positive (say, right = +). A force or velocity in the opposite direction is then negative. To combine vectors in 1D, you simply add the signed numbers.

sections.heading: Worked example: 1D vectors
sections.content:
Two forces act on a box along the same line: 5 N to the right and 3 N to the left. Taking right as positive:

$\text{resultant} = (+5) + (-3) = \mathbf{+2}$ N — i.e. 2 N to the right.

sections.heading: 8. Resultant of forces
sections.content:
The resultant force (or net force) is the single force that has the same effect as all the forces added together (as vectors).
  • If the forces are balanced, the resultant is zero → the object stays still or keeps moving at constant velocity (Newton's 1st law).
  • If the forces are unbalanced, there is a non-zero resultant → the object accelerates in the direction of the resultant (Newton's 2nd law).

sections.heading: Worked example: resultant force
sections.content:
A box is pushed with 10 N to the right while friction pushes 4 N to the left. The up/down forces (weight and normal) are balanced.

Horizontal resultant $= (+10) + (-4) = \mathbf{+6}$ N to the right, so the box accelerates to the right.

sections.heading: 9. Weight, normal force and friction
sections.content:
Three forces show up again and again (see the diagram above):
  • Weight (W) — gravity, always straight down.
  • Normal force (N) — the support force from a surface, always at 90° (perpendicular) to that surface, pushing the object away from it.
  • Friction (f) — acts along the surface, always opposing the motion (or attempted motion).

sections.heading: Hooke's Law and the limit of proportionality
sections.content:
When you stretch a spring, the extension (how much longer it gets) is proportional to the force you apply — double the force, double the extension. This is Hooke's Law.

On a force–extension graph this gives a straight line through the origin. But this only holds up to a point called the limit of proportionality. Beyond it, the spring stretches more for each extra newton and the line curves away; if pushed further the spring is permanently deformed.

sections.heading: Hooke's Law formula
sections.content: Force = spring constant × extension. Here $k$ is the spring constant (stiffness) in N/m, and $x$ is the extension in m:

sections.heading: Worked example: Hooke's Law
sections.content:
A spring has a spring constant $k = 20\text{ N/m}$ and is stretched by $x = 0.15\text{ m}$ (within its limit of proportionality).

$F = k \times x = 20 \times 0.15 = \mathbf{3}$ N.

Rearranged the other way: a force of 6 N on this spring would give $x = \frac{F}{k} = \frac{6}{20} = 0.3$ m.

sections.heading: 10. Newton's three laws of motion
sections.content:
• First law (inertia): an object stays at rest, or keeps moving at constant velocity, unless a resultant force acts on it. (Balanced forces → no change in motion.)
  • Second law: a resultant force makes a mass accelerate, following $F = m\,a$. Bigger force → bigger acceleration; bigger mass → smaller acceleration.
  • Third law: for every action there is an equal and opposite reaction. If A pushes on B, then B pushes back on A with an equal force in the opposite direction.

sections.heading: Newton's second law
sections.content: Resultant force = mass × acceleration:

sections.heading: Newton's third law in pictures
sections.content: The two forces in an action–reaction pair always act on different objects (one on A, one on B) — that is why they don't simply cancel out. They are equal in size and opposite in direction.

equal.en: equal in size, opposite in direction

sections.heading: Worked example: F = ma
sections.content:
A resultant force of 12 N acts on a 3 kg trolley. Find its acceleration.

Rearrange $F = ma$ to $a = \dfrac{F}{m} = \dfrac{12}{3} = \mathbf{4}$ m/s².

sections.heading: 11. Waves — the basics
sections.content:
A wave transfers energy from one place to another without transferring matter. Every wave is described by five quantities:
  • Amplitude (A) — the maximum displacement from the rest (equilibrium) position. Unit: metre (m). A bigger amplitude carries more energy (louder sound, brighter light).
  • Wavelength (λ) — the length of one complete wave, e.g. from one crest to the next crest (or trough to trough). Unit: metre (m).
  • Frequency (f) — the number of complete waves passing a point each second. Unit: hertz (Hz).
  • Period (T) — the time for one complete wave to pass. Unit: second (s).
  • Wave speed (v) — how fast the wave travels through the medium. Unit: m/s.
Frequency and period are opposites: $f = \frac{1}{T}$.

sections.heading: The wave equation
sections.content: Wave speed = frequency × wavelength. Frequency and period are linked by $f = 1/T$:

sections.heading: Reading wave values from a graph
sections.content:
Always check the x-axis label first — it decides what you can read:
  • Displacement–distance graph (x-axis = distance): read the wavelength (crest-to-crest spacing) and the amplitude (height of a crest).
  • Displacement–time graph (x-axis = time): read the period (time between successive crests) and the amplitude. Then get frequency from $f = 1/T$, and speed from $v = f\lambda$.
The two graphs look identical, so the axis label is the only clue to whether the spacing is a wavelength or a period.

sections.heading: Worked example: the wave equation
sections.content:
A water wave has a frequency of $50\text{ Hz}$ and a wavelength of $0.2\text{ m}$.

Speed: $v = f\lambda = 50 \times 0.2 = \mathbf{10}$ m/s.

If a displacement–time graph instead showed a period of $T = 0.02\text{ s}$, the frequency would be $f = \frac{1}{T} = \frac{1}{0.02} = 50$ Hz — the same wave.

sections.heading: 12. Transverse vs longitudinal waves
sections.content:
Waves are grouped by the direction in which they vibrate:
  • Transverse waves — the oscillation is perpendicular (at 90°) to the direction the wave travels. Examples: light (and all electromagnetic waves), water waves, waves on a string, and seismic S-waves.
  • Longitudinal waves — the oscillation is parallel to the direction of travel, squashing and stretching the medium into compressions and rarefactions. Examples: sound, ultrasound, and seismic P-waves.

sections.heading: 13. Light & the electromagnetic spectrum
sections.content:
Visible light is just one small part of the electromagnetic (EM) spectrum. The whole family shares two key facts: all EM waves are transverse, and they all travel at the same speed in a vacuum: $c = 3 \times 10^8\text{ m/s}$.

The bands run from longest wavelength / lowest frequency to shortest wavelength / highest frequency:

Radio → Microwave → Infrared → Visible → Ultraviolet → X-ray → Gamma

As you move right, wavelength gets shorter, while frequency and energy get higher.

speed.en: All EM waves travel at c = 3 × 10⁸ m/s in a vacuum

sections.heading: EM spectrum — uses and dangers
sections.content:
Each band has its own everyday uses (and some have real dangers):
  • Radio waves — broadcasting, TV, WiFi, radio communication.
  • Microwaves — cooking (microwave ovens), satellite communication, mobile phones. Danger: can heat body tissue.
  • Infrared — thermal cameras, TV remotes, night vision, heating.
  • Visible light — human vision, photography, optical fibres.
  • Ultraviolet (UV) — sun tanning, sterilisation, fluorescent lights, detecting fake banknotes. Danger: causes sunburn and skin cancer — use sunscreen.
  • X-rays — medical imaging (bones), airport security scanners. Danger: ionising — damages DNA; radiographers stand behind screens.
  • Gamma rays — cancer treatment (radiotherapy), sterilising medical equipment. Danger: ionising — damages DNA.
